titleStmt.title: Letter from Marie Martin to her mother Mary Lewis Martin, 18 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Marie Martin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Marie Martin (1892-1975) to her mother Mary Lewis Martin (née Moore). The letter refers to Marie's life and surroundings in her new posting along with the work she is doing there. She is unable to disclose her location but gives her mother clues to guess it. She concludes with a happy birthday message to her mother.Marie was later the founder of the Medical Missionaries of Mary. At the outbreak of war in 1914 Marie joined the Voluntary Aid Detachment, a division of the Red Cross. She returned home briefly in 1916 following the death of her brother in battle until she was called to serve again two months later in a field hospital in Heraldo, France.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
B/F/1/42 No 25 General Hospi. B. E. F. 18th June 1916 My very dearest Mother. We arrived here
last night about 7â30, it is
a very large Hospital but
rather quaint, it is a seaâ
side resort which has been
taken over by the Military as
a Hospital, they are sweet
little Villas & we are billeted
in some of them also the
strand is just perfect with
glorious silver sand â which
I am enjoying whilst
writing this short note â I
do not think we could have
possibly been sent to a healthier
spot it is really quite like
Malta in some ways, there
are a great number of tents
& altogether we lead a very
out of door life. I was in the
ward this morning for a short
time, it is surgical but do
not know what sort of cases
yet but they all look to
me fairly well & wont be
here long. Some of the girls
seem very nice but none of
them have been here very
long. We had a great voyage out
leaving Charing Cross by mail
train & came across on
a mail boat which was
packed & we all just sat
on our luggage it was fairly
rough in parts. After having
lunch at the Bologne
Lower Hotel we all went
for a short walk round
the town â but did not
have much time as we
had to be back in
the hotel at 5 o'ckâ
to see the matron who
packed us off hereâ
I am sending you a
riddle so as you may
guess the place I am at.
1st the opposite of soft 2nd the
5th letter in the Alphabet 3rd the
name of the man whose wife
turned into a pillar of saltâ
you have got the nameâ
Before I close this I must
wish you a happy
birthday as possible under
the circumstances & every
blessing good wish & luck
I only wish I were at
home to enjoy it with
you ââ In a few days
time I shall be able
to write you a better
account of every thing
I am very tired toânight
& want to get up for
6â30 Mass toâmorrow
morning S. V. so will
close this, excuse the
way it is written but
I am in bed now &
very sleepâ With my fondest love
good wishes & many
happy returns of the
dayâ Your most loving
daughter Marie
